coredata.dc:title: Critical investigation of up-conversion and dual emission from nitrogen functionalized graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Siddique, Abu Bakar
Mukhuti, Kingshuk
Choudhury, Subhrangshu
Pramanick, Ashit Kumar
Hossain, Syed Minhaz
Ray, Mallar
coredata.dc:description: Up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) and dual-band photoluminescence (DBPL) are two widely-reported, exotic properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). However, both these phenomena can be associated with measurement artefacts. In case of excitation with monochromatic radiation derived from a white light source, the second order of the excitation or the emission signal can cause misleading impressions of UCPL and DBPL. Laser excitations can also generate spurious UCPL and/or DBPL signals due to leaking radiations. Using a spatially separated femtosecond pulsed laser, we find that in nitrogen-functionalized GQDs there is no evidence for DBPL although a real UCPL is hidden behind spurious signals.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Photoluminescence
Up-conversion
Dual-band emission
Measurement artefact

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural features of the NGQDs: (a) bright field HRTEM image, inset: the size distribution histogram; (b) SAEDP corresponding to the image in (a); (c) high magnification image; (d) XRD profile; (e) XPS wide scan spectrum; deconvoluted (f) C1s; (g) N1s spectra; and (h) EELS profile of the NGQDs.

Misleading UCPL and DBPL from NGQDs; (a) apparent UCPL under 650 nm excitation; (b) apparent DBPL upon 325 nm excitation; (c) PL spectra with and without 500 nm long pass filter in the excitation path, (d) disappearance and appearance of NIR peak under 325 nm excitation, with and without 550 nm long pass filter in the emission path. All the spectra were recorded by putting the samples in an integrating sphere.

Impact of leaking radiations from HeNe excitation laser: (a) apparent blue emission upon red excitation, PL peak at 450 nm for 632.8 nm excitation and NIR peak at ∼870 nm that appears along with the blue peak for same excitation; and (b) spectrum of the HeNe laser showing leaking high energy laser radiations.

Real UCPL form NGQD under excitation with the 800 nm fs pulsed laser; (a) PL spectra for varying excitation power; and (b) Integrated intensity as function of excitation power plotted in log-log scale.

head.ce:title: Critical investigation of up-conversion and dual emission from nitrogen functionalized graphene quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) and dual-band photoluminescence (DBPL) are two widely-reported, exotic properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). However, both these phenomena can be associated with measurement artefacts. In case of excitation with monochromatic radiation derived from a white light source, the second order of the excitation or the emission signal can cause misleading impressions of UCPL and DBPL. Laser excitations can also generate spurious UCPL and/or DBPL signals due to leaking radiations. Using a spatially separated femtosecond pulsed laser, we find that in nitrogen-functionalized GQDs there is no evidence for DBPL although a real UCPL is hidden behind spurious signals.

ce:section.ce:para: Zero-dimensional carbon nanostructures, especially the GQDs, have recently attracted a lot of research attention for their novel photo-physical features [1]. GQDs are basically small fragments of graphene which can have a variety of edge configurations or surface passivation and may be doped by a number of species. They are ideally made of sp 2 hybridized carbon only and can be composed of one or few layers of honeycomb carbon lattice. GQDs have larger lateral spread compared to their height and exhibit a variety of quantum size effects [2]. Carbon dots (CDs) on the other hand, are non-planar nanostructures and have sp 3 hybridized orbitals along with the sp 2 phase [3]. Various aspects of GQDs and CDs have been widely explored and applications in different fields ranging from biology to flexible electronics have been demonstrated [1].

A significant number of reports claim to observe UCPL for a variety of CDs and GQDs [1,4–7]. However, the credibility of UCPL has been controversial. The existence of up-conversion in GQDs with white light excitation was reported, but those claims were refuted by careful successive studies [8–10]. Wen et al. synthesized five different types of CDs and GQDs, and identified that the apparent signatures of UCPL were actually measurement artefact [11]. Furthermore, it was reported that real up-conversion in GQDs can be observed under pulsed laser excitation [10,12]. Apart from a regular blue emission band, the luminescent GQDs are widely reported to show another hump in the near infra-red (NIR) spectral region and the emission profile is termed as DBPL [13–18]. Some contemporary studies also claim that certain CD and GQD systems demonstrate both UCPL and DBPL [14,15,18,19]. While the existence of UCPL has been debated before, the occurrence of DBPL has not been questioned so far.

Observation of UCPL and DBPL in a wide variety of CDs and GQDs is indeed quite puzzling. It is hard to imagine that different 0D carbon variants are endowed with unique electronic energy structures decorated with intermediate states so as to promote UCPL and DBPL under all kinds of excitations. Since N-functionalization is reported to produce a dominant, intermediate state within the bandgap [20,21], we choose N-functionalized GQDs (NGQDs) to understand UCPL and DBPL in these systems. After careful experimentation we find that although a real UCPL can be observed in NGQDs under concentrated pulsed excitation, DBPL is associated is measurement artefact.

The structural features of the NGQDs synthesized by standard hydrothermal technique are summarized in Fig. 1 . High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of the NGQDs shown in Fig. 1a, reveal the formation of nanostructures with average diameters of 2.1 ± 0.4 nm. The selected area electron diffraction pattern (SAEDP) in Fig. 1b, consists of rings corresponding to the (002) and (100) graphitic planes, along with a diffused halo that is typical of a randomly oriented nanocrystalline sample. The high magnification HRTEM image (Fig. 1c) also reveal fringes corresponding to the (002) and (100) planes [22]. The broad but distinct graphitic peak in the x-ray diffraction (XRD) profile at ∼26° (Figure S1, supplementary information) also confirms the nanocrystalline nature of the sample [23]. The wide scan x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum shown in Fig. 1d shows the expected sharp N peak along with C and O peaks, while the deconvoluted C1s, N1s and O1s spectra (Fig. 1e, f and g) reveal the typical surface bonds present in the NGQDs [24,25].The detail explanation about the each components are given in the supplementary information (Figure S2). Fig. 1h is the electron energy loss spectrum (EELS) of the GQDs revealing peaks at 5.5 and 14.2 eV due to the π* surface plasmon and π* + σ* bulk plasmon, respectively. The positions of these peaks indicate the formation of 2D graphene layers [26,27]. The incorporation of N is confirmed by the presence of the third peak at 43.5 eV [28].

Apparently, the NGQDs exhibit UCPL and DBPL when excited by a monochromatic light derived from a xenon white light source as shown in Fig. 2 . We have observed that when our samples were excited by 650 nm, it exhibited a misleading UPCL at 451 nm, shown in Fig. 4a. In addition to that we also observe that when we excited our sample with 325 nm, then it exhibited the DBPL peaks at 449 nm and 870 nm as shown in Fig. 2b. However, both UCPL and DBPL observed here, arise due to measurement artefacts.

When the monochromator is set to excite the sample with any wavelength λ
ex , the second order diffraction peak of λ ex/2 is also present [9,12]. The apparent UCPL observed here is due to this second order excitation peak which actually induces normal PL. As shown in Fig. 2c, the UCPL disappears with the introduction of a 500 nm long-pass filter in the excitation path. Clearly, the origin of this emission is due to the excitation of the sample by the second order of λ ex/2 which gets eliminated when a 500 nm long-pass filter is used in the excitation path. Even simple visual inspection shows that the sample emits blue light upon excitation with green, yellow and red light (Figure S3a, Figure S4, supplementary information) but exhibit no visible blue emission when second order of λ ex/2 is blocked (Figure S3b, Figure S4, supplementary information). Unfortunately, such experimental errors are still widely reported (supplementary information, Table S1), although this spurious origin has been pointed out long ago [9,11,12].

As shown in Fig. 2d, the NIR PL peak disappears when a long pass 550 nm filter is introduced in the emission path indicating that the origin of this peak is closely related to the appearance of the higher energy peak roughly at half the wavelength. Since the emission from the sample is measured with another monochromator at the detection path, the observed NIR PL band also arises due to the diffraction of first emission peak. The second order diffracted line of the emitted wavelength (or even the second order of the excitation) may be wrongly interpreted as an emission feature of the sample. One problem associated with identifying the NIR peak to be the second order is that for a broadband emission, the second order diffraction does not peak exactly at double the wavelength. In fact, the shift of the second order peak from its expected value at 2λ
em , scales linearly with the full width at half maxima of the first order curve peaking at λ
em (Figure S4, supplementary information). This scaling of the shift in peak with FWHM of the first order emission is possibly a consequence of the scattering of the incident and emitted broad spectrum of light through the colloidal particles having size dispersion [29–31]. To further elaborate on this assignment of the NIR peak to second order diffraction we plotted the entire spectrum showing dual emission for varying excitation energies which consequently shifted the blue emission peak (Figure S5, supplementary information). Hence, to arrive at definitive conclusion regarding NIR PL from NGQDs, it is essential to exclude all the possibilities of identifying the second order of either the excitation or the emission with a feature of the NGQDs.

If we use a laser excitation instead of a monochromatic light derived from a white light source, the above problems should not arise. Surprisingly, when we used a 632.8 nm HeNe Laser (Lumentum, Model 1125P, 5 mW), we observed that the NGQDs apparently exhibit both UCPL and DBPL, illustrated in the spectrum (Fig. 3 a). Interestingly, the UCPL and DBPL for HeNe excitation disappeared when a 550 nm long pass filter was introduced in the excitation and the emission channels, respectively. With HeNe laser also, we observed blue emission with naked eye but it showed no visible blue emission when second order of λ ex/2 is blocked (Figure S6a and b, Figure S4, supplementary information). This observation makes it clear that the 632.8 nm HeNe laser had higher energy emissions in addition to the dominant 632.8 nm lased radiation as shown in Fig. 3b. Semiconductor lasers are known to possess relatively low intensity lines on either side of the dominant emission [32]. Due to high absorption cross section [33], and PL quantum yield of the NGQDs (up to an unbelievable 99% QY has been reported) [34], even a weak leaking excitation is able to produce an appreciable emission output. Remarkably high absorption and exceptionally high photon conversion efficiency makes the GQDs particularly susceptible to wrong interpretations for UCPL and DBPL. Therefore, to assert on UCPL or DBPL even with laser excitations, it is imperative to negate the possibilities of exciting the samples with leaking radiations.

In order to negate any possibility of leakage, we used a Ti –Sapphire, tuneable femtosecond pulsed laser (Tsunami from Spectra Physics, max power 2 W) and the measurement was done at 800 nm by using a prism at the excitation end to spatially resolve the excitation. In addition, three mirrors were used as reflectors to enhance the spatial distance between the 800 nm pulse and any other component that might be present along with 800 nm, schematic of the setup to measure the UCPL of NGQDs is shown in Figure S7 (supplementary information). It was observed that NGQDs exhibit broad UCPL signal, peaked at around 535 nm even in the presence of a filter as shown in Fig. 4a. Unlike the cases of erroneous up-conversion, the real UCPL is barely distinguishable with the naked eye due to low intensity of emission.

However, when observed with a short pass filter (700 nm) at the eyepiece, green-blue light emission becomes observable (Figure S8, supplementary information). It is worth mentioning that we observed upconverted signal only when highly concentrated laser beam (spot diameter ∼50 μm) was operated in the pulsed mode. For our sample, no UCPL was recorded for the same laser operating in continuous wave (CW) mode (with all other parameters constant). In fact, the UCPL originating from NGQD could only be detected by a highly focussed pulsed excitation and not even with an unfocussed beam. We varied the excitation power (P) form 450 mW–1600 mW and plotted the integral intensity versus P in a double-log scale. As shown in Fig. 4b, the linearized plot has a slope of 1.84, suggesting a two-photon excitation process [35]. It was also found that with 800 nm pulsed excitation, NGQDs exhibit no emission in the NIR region with a 850 nm long pass filter in the collection path. Understanding the mechanisms responsible for the real UCPL in GQDs warrant separate investigation.

We have shown that NGQDs exhibit real UCPL in blue-green region but there is no DBPL in the NIR region. The quadratic dependence of the integrated intensity on excitation power suggests two photon mechanism in NGQD systems. Misleading signatures of UCPL and DBPL can arise due to a number of measurement artefacts like, second order harmonic in case of monochromator-based excitation/emission systems or due to leaking laser radiations. These artefacts should be carefully eliminated in accounting for either UCPL or DBPL or both, for any system in general and for NGQDs in particular, because of their high absorption and emission efficiencies.
Supporting Information is available online from the Elsevier or from the author.

Abu Bakar Siddique: Conceptualization; Investigation, Formal analysis; Writing – original draft; Writing – review & editing. Kingshuk Mukhuti: Methodology; Formal analysis; Investigation, Validation, Writing - Writing – review & editing. Subhrangshu Choudhury: Investigation; Data curation, Ashit Kumar Pramanick: Investigation, Syed Minhaz Hossain: Validation, Writing – review & editing. Mallar Ray: Conceptualization; Methodology; Formal analysis; Writing – original draft; Writing – review & editing; Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Dr Bhavtosh Bansal, IISER Kolkata for his overall support and Professors Tarun Mondal and Sugata Ray and Mr. Indranath Bhowmik of IACS, Kolkata for assisting in XPS measurements. ABS acknowledges IIEST, Shibpur PhD fellowship. MR acknowledges Federico Baur Endowed Chair in Nanotechnology.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances on graphene quantum dots: from chemistry and physics to applications

M. Bacon, S.J. Bradley, T. Nann, Graphene quantum dots, Particle & Particle Systems Characterization, 31 (2014) 415-428.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Sun, Q. Xin, Y. Yang, H. Shah, H. Cao, Y. Qi, J.R. Gong, J. Li, Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots coupled with photosensitizers for one-/two-photon activated photodynamic therapy based on a FRET mechanism, Chemical communications, 54 (2018) 715-718.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: N-functionalized graphene quantum dots with ultrahigh quantum yield and large Stokes shift: efficient downconverters for CIGS solar cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Khan, J.H. Kim, N-Functionalized Graphene Quantum Dots with Ultrahigh Quantum Yield and Large Stokes Shift: Efficient Downconverters for CIGS Solar Cells, ACS Photonics, 5 (2018) 4637-4643.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Power dependence of upconversion luminescence in lanthanide and transition-metal-ion systems

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Pollnau, D.R. Gamelin, S. Luthi, H. Gudel, M.P. Hehlen, Power dependence of upconversion luminescence in lanthanide and transition-metal-ion systems, Physical Review B, 61 (2000) 3337.
